does: RUBRIC-ARROW introduces an alternating training framework that jointly optimizes a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned reward judge for pointwise evaluation in non-verifiable domains. The method addresses the core problem that absolute scoring in subjective settings is unreliable by decomposing evaluation into explicit criteria through learned rubrics. Rather than relying on frontier LLM judgments, the approach trains using only pairwise preference data via a specialized RL stage. The framework employs a probability-based scoring rule designed to reduce tie outcomes and phase-specific preference rewards, with an alternating group reward policy optimization (GRPO) scheme coordinating the joint training of both components. Experimental results demonstrate competitive accuracy in reward modeling and measurable improvements when the learned rewards guide downstream policy post-training.
connects: This work extends the lineage of rubric-based evaluation methods that decompose subjective assessment into interpretable criteria, moving beyond simpler monolithic reward models that struggle with absolute judgment. It builds on prior research in reward modeling for LLM alignment by addressing the practical limitation that frontier models are expensive and unreliable judges, particularly in subjective domains where ground truth is undefined. The paper connects to work on pairwise preference learning and Bradley-Terry-type preference aggregation by reformulating the reward-modeling problem to work with preference data rather than absolute scores. It also engages with research on reducing overconfidence and ties in learned evaluators, using probability-based scoring rules inspired by proper scoring theory. The alternating optimization approach relates to coordinate-ascent and expectation-maximization traditions in machine learning where multiple components jointly improve.
enables: RUBRIC-ARROW opens pathways for training better reward models in domains where human judgment is the only source of ground truth, enabling more scalable post-training of language models without reliance on proprietary frontier systems. The interpretable rubric component could support human oversight and debugging of reward signals, crucial for safety-critical applications. The framework's ability to work with preference data alone suggests directions for learning from weaker human feedback signals, such as comparative judgments that are easier to collect than absolute scores. Future work could extend this to multi-stage rubrics, hierarchical criteria, or dynamic rubric adaptation based on discovered failure modes. The probability-based tie-reduction mechanism might generalize to other settings where discrete evaluations create training signal problems, while the alternating training scheme could inspire similar frameworks for jointly learning other coupled evaluator-generator pairs in alignment work.
